title: Analytical Singular-Value Structure of Analytic-Continuation Kernels from Slepian Information Theory
abstract: Analytic continuation from imaginary-time Green's functions to real-frequency spectra is a central ill-posed inverse problem in quantum many-body physics. We show that the thermal kernel admits an analytical generalized singular-value structure once its purely dynamical part is separated from the statistical weight imposed by the heat bath. The dynamical kernel is the imaginary-bandwidth continuation of Slepian's finite Fourier transform and is governed by the same Sturm-Liouville algebra that yields prolate spheroidal wave functions. Fermionic and bosonic statistics then enter as gauge transformations of the frequency-space inner product, producing self-adjoint effective potentials but no numerical kernel diagonalization. The Shannon number, $N_c=β\wmax/π$, fixes the upper information capacity of this pure Laplace channel. Finally, the optimal sampling points are obtained as eigenvalues of a Legendre colleague matrix, giving a deterministic compressed-sensing grid without iterative root searches.
summary: The paper presents an analytical framework for the analytic continuation of Green's functions by treating the thermal kernel through the lens of Slepian information theory. It identifies the Shannon number as the fundamental limit for information capacity and introduces a deterministic method for optimal data sampling. This allows for high-fidelity spectral reconstruction using significantly fewer data points than traditional dense grids.
main_problem: The ill-posed inverse problem of analytic continuation, specifically reconstructing real-frequency spectra from noisy imaginary-time Green's functions in quantum many-body physics.
main_result: The paper derives an analytical singular-value decomposition for the thermal kernel and provides a deterministic, information-theoretically optimal sampling grid using a colleague-matrix construction.
method: The author applies Slepian information theory to map the thermal kernel to a finite Fourier transform problem, utilizing Sturm-Liouville algebra and Legendre colleague matrices for optimal sampling.
model_or_system: The study focuses on the Fredholm integral equation of the first kind representing the relationship between imaginary-time Green's functions and real-frequency spectra for fermionic and bosonic many-body systems.
key_observables: Real-frequency spectra A(omega), imaginary-time Green's functions G(tau), and the Shannon number Nc.
important_parameters: The Shannon number Nc = beta*omega_max/pi, the bandwidth/temperature scale c = beta*omega_max/2, and the compression factor.
main_assumptions: The method assumes the ability to separate the universal dynamical Laplace transmission factor from the statistics-dependent thermal weight.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the exponential decay of singular values (the eigenvalue cliff) around the Shannon number. Figure 2 demonstrates that reconstruction error for a four-peak Gaussian spectrum drops significantly when the number of sampling nodes reaches the Shannon scale.
paper_structure: The paper identifies the analytic continuation problem, introduces the Slepian mapping to decompose the kernel, derives the analytical SVD via Sturm-Liouville theory, proposes a deterministic sampling grid via colleague matrices, and validates the method with a numerical compression test.
why_interesting: This provides a rigorous, information-theoretic foundation for compressed sensing in many-body dynamics, offering a way to reduce the computational cost of processing large Quantum Monte Carlo datasets without losing essential spectral information.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Numerical sampling experiment; see Sec. S6 of the Supplemental Material for algorithmic details. (A) Original spectrum A(ω) made of four Gaussian peaks, shown as a func- tion of y = ω/ωmax. (B) Imaginary-time output obtained from the fermionic kernel; red points are the M = ⌈Nc⌉= 13 colleague-matrix observation nodes. (C) Relative reconstruc- tion error of G(x) as the number of observations M is varied. The sharp improvement at the Shannon scale shows that the compressed grid preserves the information transmitted by the kernel.
